title: HTTP Client
subtitle: Connect your Angular app to real APIs
description: Master Angular's HttpClient — make GET, POST, PUT and DELETE requests, handle errors gracefully, add auth headers with interceptors, and build a clean reactive data layer using Signals.
keyPoints: provideHttpClient() registers HttpClient in app.config.ts — import it once, inject everywhere
HttpClient methods (get, post, put, delete) return Observables — subscribe or use async pipe
Always handle errors with catchError() — never let HTTP failures silently break the UI
HTTP interceptors add headers, log requests, and handle 401 errors globally — no repetition
Keep all HTTP calls inside services, never in components — components only read signals
withInterceptors([]) is the modern functional interceptor API for standalone apps
The loading/data/error signal pattern gives reactive, type-safe async state management

sections.title: What Is HttpClient?
sections.content:
HttpClient is Angular's built-in service for making HTTP requests to REST APIs and static JSON files. It wraps the browser's XMLHttpRequest (or fetch, in newer versions) and returns RxJS Observables, giving you powerful operators for transforming, combining, and error-handling responses.

Key advantages over raw fetch:
• Type-safe responses — http.get<User[]>('/api/users') returns Observable<User[]>
• Automatic JSON parsing — no response.json() needed
• Interceptors — add auth headers, log requests, handle errors globally
• Testing support — HttpClientTestingModule provides a mock backend
• Progress events — track upload/download progress
• Retry and timeout — compose with RxJS operators

sections.title: Setup — provideHttpClient() in app.config.ts
sections.explanation: provideHttpClient() is called once in app.config.ts. Every service that injects HttpClient gets the same configured instance, including all registered interceptors.

sections.title: Making GET Requests
sections.content:
HttpClient.get<T>() sends a GET request and returns an Observable<T>. You must subscribe to trigger the request — Observables are lazy by default.

The generic type parameter <T> tells TypeScript the shape of the response. Angular automatically parses the JSON body into the specified type.

Common ways to consume the Observable:
• .subscribe() in a service — store the result in a signal
• async pipe in a template — Angular manages the subscription lifecycle
• firstValueFrom() — convert to a Promise for async/await patterns

Best practice: make all HTTP calls inside services, not in components.

sections.title: GET Requests — Typed Responses
sections.explanation: EMPTY is an Observable that completes immediately without emitting. Returning it from catchError stops the error from propagating while still completing the stream cleanly — the subscribe callback simply never fires.

sections.title: Using the Service in a Component
sections.explanation: The component is thin — it reads signals and calls service methods. All HTTP logic, error handling, and state management live in the service. This makes the component easy to test and the service reusable.

sections.caption: The request flows: Component → Service → Interceptors → API → back through interceptors → Service updates signals → Component template auto-updates.

sections.title: POST, PUT, PATCH and DELETE
sections.content:
HttpClient provides methods for all HTTP verbs. They all return Observables and accept a body as the second argument (for POST, PUT, PATCH).

• http.post<T>(url, body)   — create a resource
• http.put<T>(url, body)    — replace a resource entirely
• http.patch<T>(url, body)  — update specific fields
• http.delete<T>(url)       — remove a resource

The generic type T represents the response body. For endpoints that return nothing (204 No Content), use http.delete<void>() or check the response type documentation.

sections.title: POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE
sections.explanation: HttpClient automatically sets Content-Type: application/json and serialises the body object. You never need JSON.stringify() — Angular handles it. The tap() operator is great for side effects like updating a loading signal.

sections.title: HTTP Headers and Request Options
sections.content:
Some API endpoints require custom headers — an auth token, an API key, a content type, or a correlation ID. You pass them via the options object as the last argument to any HttpClient method.

HttpHeaders is immutable — every 'set' method returns a new instance rather than mutating. This is important when building headers incrementally.

Other useful request options:
• params     — query string parameters (HttpParams)
• observe    — 'body' (default), 'response' (full response with headers), 'events' (upload progress)
• responseType — 'json' (default), 'text', 'blob', 'arraybuffer'

sections.title: HTTP Headers and Request Options
sections.explanation: HttpParams and HttpHeaders are both immutable — each .set() call returns a new instance. This immutability prevents accidental mutation when headers are built across multiple method calls.

sections.title: Error Handling with catchError
sections.content:
HTTP requests fail — networks are unreliable, APIs go down, users lose connectivity. You must always handle errors explicitly.

RxJS catchError operator intercepts errors in the Observable pipeline. It receives the error and must return a new Observable.

Common error handling strategies:
• Return EMPTY         — silently stop, no further emissions
• Return of(default)  — emit a fallback value and complete
• throw new Error()   — rethrow (component catches it)
• Return timer + retry — automatic retry with backoff

Angular wraps HTTP errors in HttpErrorResponse, which contains:
• status    — HTTP status code (404, 500, 0 for network errors)
• message   — error message
• error     — the parsed error body from the API
• statusText — text like 'Not Found', 'Internal Server Error'

sections.title: Comprehensive Error Handling
sections.explanation: err.status === 0 means a network error — no response reached the server (offline, CORS, DNS failure). Always check for status 0 separately from HTTP error codes.

sections.content: Use the retry() operator with a delay for GET requests on unstable networks — it is safe to retry idempotent requests. Never auto-retry POST/PUT/DELETE — the request may have reached the server and succeeded before the response was lost, causing duplicate actions.

sections.title: HTTP Interceptors — Global Request/Response Handling
sections.content:
Interceptors sit in the middleware pipeline between your service and the HTTP transport. Every request passes through all registered interceptors before being sent, and every response passes back through them on the way back.

Common use cases:
• Auth interceptor  — append Bearer token to every request automatically
• Logging interceptor — log request URL, method, status, and duration
• Error interceptor — catch 401 errors globally and redirect to login
• Loading interceptor — show a global spinner for all in-flight requests
• Cache interceptor  — return a cached response for repeated GET requests

Modern Angular (17+) uses functional interceptors — just a function, no class needed.

sections.title: Auth Interceptor — Add Bearer Token
sections.explanation: HttpRequest is immutable — req.clone() creates a new request with the specified changes. Never mutate req directly. The req.url.includes() check prevents sending your auth token to third-party APIs.

sections.title: Logging Interceptor — Measure Request Time
sections.explanation: finalize() runs regardless of success or error — perfect for timing. The error interceptor handles 401/403 globally so no individual service has to. throwError() re-throws so the service's catchError still fires.

sections.title: Transforming Responses with RxJS Operators
sections.content:
HttpClient returns raw Observables — you can chain RxJS operators to transform, filter, or combine the data before it reaches your signal.

Most useful operators with HTTP:
• map()         — transform the response shape
• tap()         — side-effects (logging, analytics) without changing the stream
• switchMap()   — chain requests (search-as-you-type: cancel previous)
• forkJoin()    — parallel requests — wait for all to complete
• combineLatest()— react to multiple sources (user + permissions)
• shareReplay() — cache and share one HTTP response to multiple subscribers
• debounceTime()— wait for user to stop typing before firing a search request

sections.title: Chaining Requests and Parallel Requests
sections.explanation: forkJoin is ideal for loading multiple independent data sources for a dashboard — three API calls fire in parallel instead of sequentially, cutting load time to max(t1, t2, t3) instead of t1+t2+t3.

sections.content: Always unsubscribe from long-lived Observables when a component is destroyed to prevent memory leaks. For HTTP requests this is less critical (they complete after one response), but for continuous streams use takeUntilDestroyed() from @angular/core/rxjs-interop — it automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed.

sections.title: Complete CRUD Service Example
sections.explanation: Optimistic updates change the local signal immediately for instant UI feedback, then sync with the server. On server failure, the change is rolled back. This makes the app feel fast on slow networks.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you set up HttpClient in a standalone Angular app?
interviewQuestions.a: Call provideHttpClient() in the providers array of ApplicationConfig in app.config.ts. Optionally pass feature functions: withInterceptors([...]) for functional interceptors, withFetch() to use the Fetch API instead of XMLHttpRequest. This registers HttpClient as a singleton — any service or component can then inject it with inject(HttpClient). Never import HttpClientModule in standalone apps — that is the legacy NgModule approach.

interviewQuestions.q: Why do you need to subscribe to HttpClient methods? What happens if you don't?
interviewQuestions.a: RxJS Observables are lazy — they do not execute until a subscriber is attached. HttpClient.get() returns an Observable that represents the HTTP request, but the request is not actually sent until you call .subscribe() or convert it with firstValueFrom(). If nothing subscribes, no HTTP request is ever made. The async pipe in templates is a form of subscription that also automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed.

interviewQuestions.q: What is HttpErrorResponse and how do you handle different error types?
interviewQuestions.a: HttpErrorResponse is the error object Angular provides in the catchError operator. Key fields: status (HTTP code — 0 for network errors, 4xx for client errors, 5xx for server errors), message, error (the parsed API error body), and statusText. Status 0 means no response reached the server — network offline, CORS error, or DNS failure. In catchError, check err.status to provide user-friendly messages: 401 → 'Not logged in', 404 → 'Not found', 500+ → 'Server error', 0 → 'No internet connection'.

interviewQuestions.q: What is an HTTP interceptor and what are common use cases?
interviewQuestions.a: An interceptor is middleware that processes every HTTP request before it is sent and every response before it reaches the service. Modern Angular uses functional interceptors: export const myInterceptor: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => { ... return next(req); }. Register them with withInterceptors([myInterceptor]) in provideHttpClient(). Common uses: automatically adding Authorization headers (auth interceptor), logging request timing (logging interceptor), globally redirecting on 401 errors (error interceptor), showing a global loading spinner (loading interceptor), and caching GET responses.

interviewQuestions.q: Why is HttpRequest immutable and how do you modify a request in an interceptor?
interviewQuestions.a: HttpRequest is immutable to prevent accidental shared-state bugs when multiple interceptors modify the same request. To modify a request, call req.clone({ ... }) with the changes — it returns a new HttpRequest with those changes applied and all other properties copied from the original. Common changes in clone: setHeaders, params, url, body, withCredentials. Pass the cloned request to next(clonedReq) instead of the original.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between switchMap and forkJoin when chaining HTTP requests?
interviewQuestions.a: forkJoin fires multiple requests in parallel and emits one combined result when ALL complete — ideal for loading independent data sources simultaneously (like a dashboard). switchMap chains sequential requests where the result of the first feeds into the second, and importantly cancels the previous inner request if the outer observable emits again — ideal for search-as-you-type (typing a new character cancels the previous search request). mergeMap is like switchMap but does not cancel, allowing parallel inner requests.

interviewQuestions.q: What is shareReplay and when would you use it with HTTP?
interviewQuestions.a: shareReplay(1) caches the last emitted value and replays it to any new subscriber, also sharing a single subscription to the source. For HTTP, this means only one network request is made even if multiple components subscribe. It is useful for rarely-changing data like app configuration, feature flags, or user profile — instead of making the same HTTP call from multiple places, define the observable once at service level with shareReplay(1) and share it.

interviewQuestions.q: What is an optimistic update and how do you implement it with signals?
interviewQuestions.a: An optimistic update applies the change to the local UI state immediately (before the server confirms it), giving instant feedback. If the server call fails, you revert the change. Implementation: 1) Update the signal immediately. 2) Send the HTTP request. 3) In catchError, undo the signal change (or re-fetch). This makes the app feel fast even on slow networks — the user sees the result instantly instead of waiting for the round-trip.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you send custom headers with an HttpClient request?
interviewQuestions.a: Create an HttpHeaders instance and pass it in the options object: const headers = new HttpHeaders({ 'Authorization': 'Bearer ' + token }); return this.http.get('/api/data', { headers }). HttpHeaders is immutable — use new HttpHeaders({ key: value }) for initial values, or chain .set('key', 'value') calls (each returns a new instance). In practice, add auth headers globally via an interceptor rather than in every service method.

interviewQuestions.q: Why should HTTP calls be in services rather than components?
interviewQuestions.a: Keeping HTTP calls in services provides: reusability (multiple components can use the same service method), testability (mock the service in component tests without needing HttpClientTestingModule), separation of concerns (components describe the UI, services handle data), and single source of truth (one service instance serves all components that need the same data). If a component makes its own HTTP calls, that data is local and cannot be shared; if two components need the same data, you make two network requests. Services with reactive signals solve all of this.
